data.title: MSC Industrial Direct's Dip After Earnings Isn't A Reason To Buy
data.description:
Frankly, I find myself a bit surprised today. After reporting financial results for the second quarter of its 2026 fiscal year, MSC Industrial Direct (MSM) saw its share price drop around 0.8%. I am not surprised that it declined during a day when the market was up nicely. In fact, I am surprised that the stock didn't fall further. Revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share, all fell short of what analysts were hoping to see. However, the market was likely optimistic about the fact that, year over year, the company is doing quite well. Shares are not what I would consider to be expensive at this time, but they aren't cheap enough to fall into the undervalued category as I typically describe it. That's why I believe that calling it a "Hold" makes the most sense here.

That is what I had the company rated when I last wrote about it in April of last year. Whenever I call a company a "Hold," I am making the claim that the stock should perform similar to the broader market for the foreseeable future. In the time that has passed since then, MSC Industrial Direct has outperformed a bit, rising 22.1% while the S&P 500 is up 17.2%. But because of the strong fundamental performance that the business has exhibited, I can understand some degree of outperformance here. I don't think, however, that the company is worthy of change in my overall rating based on its valuation.

Disappointment across the board

Shares of MSC Industrial Direct ticked slightly lower on April 1st after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. This follows management reporting revenue and profitability that fell short of expectations. However, you should not conflate this with the overall health of the business. In fact, the company, which operates as a leading North American distributor of metalworking, maintenance, repair, and operations, not to mention production fastener and hardware products and services, is doing quite well. For those not familiar with the scale of the enterprise, it operates an entire network of assets that includes 37 warehouses, 5 manufacturing locations, 9 regional inventory centers, and other assets, all dedicated to selling the 2.5 million SKUs that the firm makes available to customers through its e-commerce channels, catalogs, brochures, and more.

Revenue for the quarter ended up being $917.8 million. That translated to a rise of 2.9% compared to the $891.7 million reported a year earlier. According to management, this increase in revenue was the result of $58.8 million of increased prices that the firm charged its customers, as well as a $2.9 million benefit associated with foreign currency fluctuations. Revenue would have been higher had it not been for the fact that volumes pushed sales lower to the tune of $35.6 million. Management claimed that this drop in revenue was driven in part by weather-related conditions and the partial government shutdown, the latter of which pushed public sales down by 1% year over year. Interestingly, management made the case that instead of seeing demand slow down, they are actually seeing demand increase. That seems to be at odds with broader economic conditions, and I have to wonder whether this is just because of a better position that the company has put itself in from a customer relationship and competitive standpoint.

The revenue reported by management happened to be $13.7 million below what analysts were hoping to see. But that wasn't the only area of weakness on a relative basis. Year over year, earnings per share grew from $0.70 to $0.76. But that ended up still being $0.07 lower than what analysts were hoping for. Despite this, it still translated to a rise in net profits from $39.3 million to $42.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the company fell short of expectations also. Adjusted earnings per share rose from $0.72 to $0.82. However, that still ended up being $0.02 below what had been guessed by analysts. Regardless, that still meant a rise in adjusted net income from $40.4 million to $45.8 million. The first chart in this article shows certain cash flow metrics as well. These all improved year over year.

This seems to be part of a broader trend for the company. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue, profits, and two of the three cash flow metrics that I calculated for the business all improved year over year. Adjusted net income increased also. According to management, higher prices added $97.8 million to the top line, while foreign currency contributed another $3.9 million. However, sales volumes are still down, having dropped $38.4 million year over year. This overall decline in volume does suggest to me that while management said that demand is picking up, it is actually quite weak. This does conform with my own view that the economy seems to be heading toward a recession.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety, we don't have a great deal of information to go from to guess what the picture might look like. Management did say that the firm should see some growth in the third quarter. This is even though geopolitical issues, such as the war against Iran and rising fuel costs, have created some uncertainty for the company. There also seem to be risks when it comes to certain cost inputs, such as carbide, which has seen a surge in pricing. Although management has been taking steps to ensure ample supply, carbide itself has been a bit of an issue that has created some supply constraints for the business. Beyond this, however, we are left a little bit in the dark.

The easiest way to value the company would be to project out results based on what we have seen already for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year. Assuming that this trend continues, adjusted net profits should grow from $210 million last year to $239.6 million this year. Adjusted operating cash flow should expand from $329.4 million to $361.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business should increase from $404.4 million to $440.9 million.

If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. On an absolute basis, I would definitely say that the business is not expensive. But it's not in the cheap category either. It sits perhaps near the lower end of the fair value range. Relative to other similar companies, on the other hand, it is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In that same chart, you can see precisely what I mean here. On a price-to-earnings basis, it is cheaper than any of these five businesses. And when it comes to the other two approaches to valuing the company, just two of the five enterprises ended up being cheaper than it is.

Since I prioritize absolute valuation over relative valuation, I naturally would gravitate toward the more conservative approach to valuing the company. But this does not mean that I am anything other than optimistic about it from an operational standpoint in the long haul. Management has a long history of growing the business. For instance, from 2022 through 2025, the firm deployed about $1.5 billion in capital. 23% of this was growth-oriented capital expenditures. Another 7% involved mergers and acquisitions activities. Having said that, I would be derelict in my duties if I did not acknowledge that most of the company's efforts have gone toward returning cash to shareholders. 47% of what the company deployed during this time went to dividends. Another 23% was allocated towards share buybacks.

That trend has continued throughout this year. In the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, the company paid out $97.2 million in dividends to shareholders. It also brought back 160,000 shares of itself in exchange for $13.7 million. I suspect that, throughout the rest of this year, the company will continue to deploy capital in this manner. Given how the stock is priced, my strong preference would be to focus on growth. But if a significant ramping up in growth is not in the cards, then I would prefer some share buybacks over higher dividends. In my view, dividends only make sense when there are literally no other sources of capital that are productive and when shares are very expensive.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I must say that I remain optimistic about MSC Industrial Direct from an operational standpoint. But unfortunately, I cannot in good faith turn bullish on it. That has nothing to do with the fact that management fell short of expectations that analysts had for it. Rather, it stems from the fact that the stock looks more fairly valued than undervalued at this time. Because of this, I will maintain my "Hold" rating on the stock.

data.title: OpenAI Won't Save ARKK
data.description:
As SpaceX (SPACE), Anthropic (ANTHRO), and OpenAI (OPENAI) seemingly race to go public, a trend of ETFs and other funds trying to get exposure to these private ventures pre-IPO has emerged.

I've covered one of these recently, the XOVR ETF, and found that position liquidity was a problem. SpaceX exposure would fluctuate from <2% to >10% due to new AUM not being able to buy into private vehicles fast enough to keep up but being able to deploy into public stocks easily. So while the fund itself still has an interesting premise, its execution isn't what I had envisioned, and I downgraded it to hold. That hasn't stopped other funds from trying a similar tactic of including private stock.

Enter Cathie Wood's ARK Invest and their flagship ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), which I last covered in an article titled “Trading into the Wind,” which has been pining to add private exposure to its holdings since the fairly successful launch of the ARK Venture Fund (ARKVX).

Now that these major private companies are nearing their expected IPOs, ARKK is jumping in with a new addition: OpenAI. But I don't believe it adds to the fund; in fact, I believe it could be a drag for ARKK investors, especially since it is being given tremendous weight in the fund. It further solidifies my Hold rating; ARKK will not outperform the market even with this change.

ARKK Overview

Briefly, here are the statistics for ARKK that investors should be aware of:

Inception: October 2014
Price: $68.50
Div Freq: Annual
Div Rate: ~0.80%
AUM: $5.97B
ER: 0.75%
Holdings: 45

For those new to ARKK, I wrote the educational piece on ARKK for Seeking Alpha, where I covered its history and the kinds of risks that investors should know when investing in this fund.

Here's a relevant excerpt from that article that gives some context on ARKK for our purposes today:

[ARKK] invests in a wide swath of ideas, aiming to hold the "best of the best" stocks in each of its categories. It is willing to hold on to some big losers because some of its big winners often outpace them, or at least that's the idea. To win, one necessarily has to sometimes lose, as the mindset of this kind of investing approach goes.

Naturally, that makes the innovation bet a speculative venture, and is largely suitable for aggressive investors only...

ARK Invest offers the following fund description on their website that provides their definition of innovation, as every fund that offers this kind of speculative approach has its own definitions for what is and isn't "innovation."

"ARKK defines "disruptive innovation" as the introduction of a technologically enabled new product or service that potentially changes the way the world works.

Companies within ARKK include those that rely on or benefit from the development of new products or services, technological improvements, and advancements in scientific research relating to the areas of:

  • Intelligent Devices
  • Autonomous Mobility
  • Precision Therapies
  • Neural Networks
  • Next Gen Cloud
  • Digital Wallets
  • Digital Assets
  • Smart Contracts
  • Multiomic Technologies."

ChatGPT, Meet ARKK

In the ARK strategy, which I've long called “the shotgun approach,” what is currently hot is what the next move is. So it wasn't long before private assets started to make their way into ARKK. Given how hot they've been in the last year, and with the upcoming big three AI firms (SpaceX owns xAI now) debuting this year, liquidity may be less of an issue.

It came as little surprise to me when I saw the news of ARK announcing that they will include a ~3% allocation to OpenAI in their flagship ARKK as well as their fintech (ARKF) and internet (ARKW) funds.

That announcement was on March 31st, and as of April 1st, the ARKK holdings now reflect a ~3% position in OpenAI—specifically “PPOPENAIC”—which I'm understanding to mean “private placement OpenAI series C.”

The CUSIP makes it clear that this is the Series C fundraising round, which was conducted earlier that day.

It's been clear in my past analyses of ARKK that I am not a fan of their premise, stock selection criteria, or trading behavior. But I think that the inclusion of OpenAI makes ARKK worse off than it was before.

Admittedly, OpenAI does fit the general premise of most of the stocks in the portfolio. It has several things in common with their largest holdings:

  1. The company's focus is on the bleeding edge of a hot technology.
  2. They have enormous growth potential.
  3. They trade at extreme valuation metrics; the fund's average PE is ~50.
  4. They are highly unprofitable (largely the cause of #3).

But I don't believe that frontier labs, the term for pure-play AI model providers like OpenAI, xAI, and Anthropic, will be the real winners of the AI rush. They produce a commodity that people have little loyalty or fealty toward, instead preferring the cheapest and best-performing model. So a bet on OpenAI is a bet that ChatGPT will continue to dominate market share and be the eventual winner of the AI model competitions.

I argue that OpenAI is not only overvalued in its current form but worth more dead than alive.

The Problem With OpenAI

Participating in the Series C means that ARKK just bought at the highest valuation OpenAI has ever had, now $852B after this funding round, which was also participated in by SoftBank (SFTBY), Nvidia (NVDA), and Amazon (AMZN).

Here's some context on that figure:

But unlike those firms on the right, whose valuations still don't add up to OpenAI's, they are profitable. OpenAI isn't, and not only that, it's currently on track to burn $218B over the next three years. That's more than 10x what Uber (UBER) burned in its path toward profitability.

I understand that sometimes you have to spend money to make money, but I tend to apply that to CapEx purchases. OpenAI is mostly spending this on OpEx, just burning money to run ChatGPT and the other AI models.

We know that even their highest-tier paid option is unprofitable, and their Sora video generation app was so deeply unprofitable that they had to pull the plug on it despite a $1B investment from Disney (DIS), which died with the app. There are now reports that “adult mode” is on pause as well.

It could be a rejiggering of business interests toward intelligence, which is the claim from OpenAI (although I am suspicious of an artificial intelligence company that frames its older business decisions as a shift away from intelligence), or they could be burning cash even faster than expected.

I expect these issues to get worse, especially as ChatGPT loses its scaling benefits. Its market share is shrinking as it loses users to other models. Most recently, Anthropic's Claude has gained significant popularity due to its ongoing spat with the government.

Among enterprise customers, whom I expect to be the largest source of real revenue for these frontier labs, was losing market share from well before then. The paradigm shift started in January, when Anthropic overtook OpenAI in first-timer acquisitions.

To be clear, OpenAI is still the leading AI model provider among businesses. But its early lead from the media attention it received back when it first launched publicly is fading, and the effects of leveraging money now to scale later are looking harder to achieve than they used to.

Anthropic, Google (GOOGL) with its Gemini model, and xAI's Grok model are all on the rise, while OpenAI's market share stagnates.

I am not bullish on any frontier labs and believe that the gains in AI will go to the existing hyperscaler cloud providers while they lease models from these labs and leverage their ownership of the data centers to keep margins high and cash flowing despite their own internal burn spent on LLM tokens.

Ultimately, I expect the large hyperscalers to buy up these model companies once they can no longer sustain the massive cash burns, and profitability stays ever far away. OpenAI is likely worth more dead than alive, and so I'll be avoiding exposure to it in the meantime.

Conclusion

I am maintaining my Hold rating on ARKK and believe that it will not outperform the market; I suspect it may even see negative returns as the large allocation to OpenAI will drag on its returns moving forward.

The inclusion of OpenAI into the ARK funds does not bode well for ARKK's future returns, as it is a firm that looks to be overvalued, unprofitable, and losing the only thing that makes it a viable business: total control of market share. Warning signs are already showing up, with products launched and invested in by serious partners being canceled and a cash burn plan that totals over $200B.

ARKK may be trying to stir up investor interest, and it seems to have worked, as they got headlines about the investment. But investors should be warned that this does not seem to be a prudent investment or one that will pay off anytime soon for ARKK or its investors.

This inclusion is not a complete poison pill for investors who were already bullish on the fund, but it should be a consideration. Even if OpenAI isn't in line, it's likely that more unprofitable model providers will be added to the fund in the future. I would expect Anthropic, which is already held by the AGIX ETF, among others, to be added soon.

An even heavier weighting to frontier model providers would cause me to re-evaluate my position to a sell, most likely.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Urban Outfitters: Nuuly Offers An Overlooked Growth Engine, Backed By A Debt-Free Balance Sheet
data.description:
Introduction

The first time I covered Urban Outfitters (URBN), I called them “A Debt-Free Retailer With Growing Brands And Improving Fundamentals,” highlighting their excellent financials and significant potential to expand in the long-term while navigating near-term macro pressure quite well.

After an overall solid year where the company continued their expansion despite macro headwinds, URBN remains a Buy, being backed by very solid fundamentals, with zero debt and strong brands that can continue their long-term expansion thanks especially to Nuuly, while CAPEX is expected to ramp up even more now thanks to significant investments.

Expansion Continues, Foundation Still Spotless

Despite record holiday sales, URBN missed the market’s EPS expectations while beating on revenue, with significant growth in nearly everything, posting strong growth in most segments of the business while their Nuuly subscription segment grew at a very strong 43% during the quarter, with gross profit improving to a record $599 million during Q4 despite macro weakness and tariff pressure.

URBN’s free cash flow reached a solid $315 million in FY26 (almost identical to the $320 million seen in FY25) despite a significant increase in CAPEX, which is not bad for a ~$5.47 billion market cap given their aggressive expansion, tariff impacts, and overall weak macro environment, potentially being significantly better in case of a better environment.

For FY27, URBN expects to grow their number of stores to 836 (opening 57, closing 14), while CAPEX is expected to jump a lot, planning to spend approximately $385 million, with ~40% going into retail store expansion and support, ~40% into their investments in logistics, and ~20% into technology investments and home office expansion.

Note that their guidance is before the latest tariff announcements, expecting high single-digit growth in total company sales, with mid-single-digit retail segment comps and still mid-double-digit revenue growth at Nuuly even before the tariff reversal.

Financially, based on URBN’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with virtually no debt and current assets that cover their current liabilities well, as well as $369 million in cash and cash equivalents and $327 million in marketable securities. This is ultimately an advantage of URBN, as their lack of debt combined with the fact that they rely on leasing properties can add significant potential to expand while also being more flexible in terms of closing underperforming operations.

URBN's buybacks also nearly tripled last year, completing $154 million worth of buybacks, representing a yield of roughly 2.8% based on current prices, although that’s a very manageable payout ratio of ~49%.

Macro Risks Rising

To start on a positive note, we saw the Supreme Court’s positive tariffs decision, which helped clear some headwinds and uncertainty surrounding the company’s margins, and the COO highlighted a potential upside opportunity of 25 basis points in gross profit margins if the current tariff environment remains, also mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Now on to the current year. The recently announced Supreme Court ruling and subsequent Section 122 announcement certainly change things. These recent events were not contemplated in our original plans for fiscal year 2027, which Melanie is going to talk about in a few minutes. If the Section 122 tariffs stay in place for the year or expire in July, we do believe there could be an incremental IMU benefit to our current plans. There are certainly a lot of external discussions about what will or won't happen over the coming months. So we are cautious about planning for change until there is more clarity. In the meantime, our teams continue to work diligently on all our tariff mitigation efforts.

On top of that, the ongoing Iran conflict can have detrimental effects on the company’s near-term prospects (at least), posing a risk for the demand given the effects on discretionary budgets - as pump prices jumped significantly from day one - while central banks warned about the potential inflationary pressure that can lead to higher interest rates, even though we see the market’s optimism in a potential resolution rise recently.

Again, even though Urban Outfitters is in an arguably strong position and shouldn’t be impacted by higher debt costs that much, we’re still talking about something that can have effects on their clients, partners, and especially the market’s valuation, with worries about this inflation and the Fed’s potential response already putting pressure on their stock price, as treasury yields could also become even more attractive, “forcing” the market to adjust these valuations to a risk-adjusted level given how the yield on treasuries skyrocketed in March.

Still, this company can also benefit from a potential long-term recovery while their Nuuly subscription service continues to grow aggressively, and I’ll mention again that it can offer a very significant advantage that other peers don’t seem to work on.

Ultimately, this can generate very valuable data about their consumers alongside broader trends, generating data about brand power, preferences, design issues, etc. that should allow them to do something similar to what retailers are doing now in the food industry - tailoring their offering to fill in the missing gaps in demand through data analytics, building a potential powerhouse.

Risks Reiterated

Besides trends and other fashion-related industry risks, URBN is always going to depend on the consumer, and given the ongoing macro uncertainty, there’s definitely the risk of seeing an even weaker environment, at least in the foreseeable future, potentially hurting the company’s expansion plans and growth expectations.

Meanwhile, competitive pressure remains very significant, and investors shouldn’t forget that we’re talking about a company that’s still trading at just a fraction of what the industry’s titans trade at - and that’s just the legal side of the competition.

Valuation

For URBN's DCF to Equity, with zero net borrowings given their spotless balance sheet, assuming $385 million in CAPEX, I'll assume they can deliver a FCF of roughly $240 million in 2026 (i.e. their FY’27), which adding back $125 million from 2027 (FY’28) - meaning the CAPEX would fall back to the $260 million seen last year, which is relatively elevated - on top of a 10% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their significant investments, buybacks boosting the per share amounts, and potential for macro improvements.

I’ll assume this is followed by a 7.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 10% to take into account the competitive pressure and demand a good margin of safety, with 91.35 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $6.92 billion, for an intrinsic value of $75.77, which is not far from the levels seen even a few months ago and above the current $62.67 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

URBN remains a Buy, backed by a spotless balance sheet and very significant investments planned for this year, growing well despite the ongoing macro headwinds and standing to benefit in the long-term.

Competition and the economic uncertainty remain significant risks, but the current valuation offers a solid margin of safety, implying quite conservative assumptions already.

data.title: Starbucks: The Financial Brew Sours
data.description:
Starbucks Corp. (SBUX), the world's largest coffee-shop chain, showed signs of a top-line recovery that fueled a February rally, but the fundamental picture remains bleak. A soft rebound in revenue has come at the expense of profit margins, offering little justification for the stock’s rich valuation.

Considering both fundamental and macro headwinds, I initiate coverage with a sell rating and a price target of $42.40, which implies a potential downside of about 50%. With skepticism of a sustained recovery, I apply a 20x forward earnings multiple versus ~39x currently traded.

Margin squeeze

The company eked out 4% comparable sales growth in the latest quarter, including a 3% transaction increase and a 1% average ticket gain. CEO Brian Niccol, who previously steered the successful turnaround of restaurant chain Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), has implemented the “Back to Starbucks” strategy to improve service and cut wait times. However, rising costs are making this strategy less sustainable.

Gross margin shrank to 21.1% in 1Q26, which ended December 2025, from 24.5% a year ago, while operating margin similarly compressed by 290 bps to 9%. Bloated labor costs, driven by more baristas and their training, and higher tech spend to streamline operations have put a squeeze on profitability. As a result, adjusted EPS dropped 19% YoY to 56 cents in the quarter. A sharp decline in GAAP-based earnings was largely attributable to the reclassification of SBUX’s China assets following a stake sale, which led to a surge in recorded tax liabilities.

While the company guides for a 3%-plus increase in 2026 comparable revenue, along with a slight improvement in operating margin, I doubt both goals are achievable at the same time. They can’t have the cake and eat it. For FY26, which ends September 2026, I expect adjusted EPS to stay flat at $2.12, below the low end of SBUX’s guidance range ($2.15-$2.40).

Macro headwinds

Externally, US consumer sentiment has soured into 2026, as high oil prices and AI-driven job losses weigh on household finance. Signs of weakness have emerged across the consumer discretionary spectrum, from fashion retailing to casual dinning, to big-ticket durable goods.

Starbucks sits in a particularly exposed position. A $7 cold brew with oat milk and foam is among the skippable items when purse strings are tightened. Brewing coffee at home is a straightforward and cheap substitute.

Meanwhile, the competitive landscape has shifted against SBUX both in the US and internationally. In America, Dutch Bros (BROS) has been expanding its drive-through model with aggressive pricing and a loyal young customer base. Independent local shops are also luring people with their customization and a sense of community.

In Asia, the picture is arguably worse. Luckin Coffee (LKNCY) has become China's largest coffee chain by store count and is still growing its revenue at double digits. Luckin's aggressive pricing, digital-first model, and integration with China's food delivery platforms have eroded SBUX’s position in what was once its most exciting growth market.

There are other headaches besides rising competition. Hybrid and remote work arrangements have reduced the daily commute ritual that once drove SBUX’s morning traffic. And the “third place” function of coffee shops - a social anchor between home and the office - is on the wane.

In addition, the rise of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, which reduce sugar craving, poses a threat to the company’s high-margin products. At risk are syrup-loaded Frappuccinos, whipped cream toppings, cold foam, and pastries — the add-ons that drive ticket size and margin.

Valuation

Let’s examine how Starbucks stacks up against one of its aggressive rivals. Luckin’s revenue is forecast to rise ~20% this year, following a 43% expansion in 2025, compared with SBUX’s low-single-digit growth. On a key measure of capital efficiency, Luckin’s return on invested capital has stayed in the high-teens region for three years, while SBUX’s ratio has fallen below 7%, near its weighted average cost of capital. An ROIC below cost of capital would suggest a company generates no economic value for investors. Despite higher growth and efficiency, Luckin is trading at around 16x estimated 2026 earnings, compared with ~39x for SBUX.

There’s no doubt that Starbucks remains a strong global brand with a loyal customer base and unmatched footprints across more than 80 countries and regions. I therefore apply a 20x forward earnings multiple, acknowledging the company's strengths while accounting for its weak growth, margin pressure, and structural headwinds. Combining this ratio with my $2.12 estimate for 2026 EPS yields a target price of $42.40, implying a roughly 50% downside.

Key risks

Brian Niccol has a formidable track record of rebuilding Chipotle and transforming it into one of the highest-performing restaurant chains. If his "Back to Starbucks" strategy proves more successful than I expect, through stimulating transaction growth while absorbing costs, the current SBUX valuation will become more defensible.

I could also be wrong about the US consumption outlook. If the job market improves and the oil crisis quickly recedes, Starbucks will find it easier to lure customers back while holding or raising prices.

Conclusion

Starbucks is caught between a rock and a hard place. While the "Back to Starbucks" strategy shows signs of reviving sales, an accompanying margin squeeze raises doubts about its sustainability. Macro headwinds, rising competition and the potential threat of weight-loss drugs all compound the challenge. The company’s valuation looks overstretched under these circumstances, and I apply a much lower earnings ratio to the stock, along with a sell rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Expect SCHD's Stellar Performance To Eventually Give Way To Inferior Returns
data.description:
In early January of this year, I made a rather bold claim that buying Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) would be an awful way to start 2026. To be honest with you, that has not aged well. Or at least, it hasn't aged well up to this point. From the time that article was published until the close of day on March 28th, the S&P 500 (SP500) was down 6.9%. By comparison, the total return of SCHD was up 10.7%. In the short run, this outperformance has been decisive. However, it does echo a previous article that I had written about the ETF last year.

In April of 2025, I warned that SCHD would continue to be a bitter disappointment to investors in all scenarios other than the world falling apart. Fast forward to this year, and that seems to be happening. In the U.S., economic conditions are deteriorating thanks to tariffs and high interest rates. Consumer confidence is near all-time lows. Jobs data is looking worse and worse month after month. And now we have this war with Iran that is creating geopolitical uncertainty and rising oil prices that will further hurt the U.S. This helps to explain why SCHD, which is bolstered by its distributions and the fact that it's overall slate of assets are relatively low risk, outperformed the market leading up to this point.

In the near term, this outperformance is likely to continue. I do think that economic conditions are worsening. And as I argued back in August of last year, the U.S. does seem to be nearing a recession. If economic conditions deteriorate, we could see SCHD deliver quite nicely. However, my focus when it comes to investing is not on the short run. Rather, it is the long term outlook. And when you look at the picture through that lens, SCHD still certainly deserves a Sell rating.

Checking in on SCHD

At first glance, SCHD might seem to be an attractive opportunity for investors. The current yield that it offers right now stands at 3.45%. That's not fantastic, but it's not nothing either. And with interest rates declining, companies and ETFs that previously offered lower yields are suddenly becoming more attractive than they were when interest rates were higher. One thing that certainly helps here is that a lot of the yield that it generates comes from the energy sector. In the chart above, you can see the breakdown of industries that it is exposed to. 19.88% of its assets are tied up in energy plays. And with oil prices (CL1:COM) rising through the roof right now, it stands to reason that this part of its portfolio would be performing exceptionally well.

For context, its largest holding is none other than Chevron Corporation (CVX). It accounts for 4.70% of its assets. Year to date, that stock is up 39.5% because of the surge in oil prices. That's not even the best performer. Its second largest holding is ConocoPhillips (COP), which is even more exposed to changes in oil prices. Shares of that energy play are up 42.6% year to date. If this situation with Iran continues to escalate, further upside for these names and others in its portfolio is probable.

Of course, the return of the ETF is based on returns across all of its assets on a weighted basis. That's why the stock is not up more than it currently is. Consumer staples, for instance, account for about 18.50% of its portfolio. Healthcare amounts to 16.20%. And industrial stocks total 12.10%. We also have meaningful exposure when it comes to financials, consumer discretionary companies, and information technology.

Even though SCHD has outperformed the S&P 500, as well as other major market indices, since I last wrote about it, that does not mean that it has outperform them over the long haul. In fact, over most windows of time, it has indeed been horribly disappointing. In the chart above, you can see that outperformance really began sometime around July of 2025. That's around the time that market volatility started creating issues for market participants. But prior to that point, SCHD has been a loser compared to the S&P 500, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, and the NASDAQ. The most telling example is from the time that I originally rated it a "sell" back in September of 2023. From that time through the time of this writing, the ETF is up 37.4%. The S&P 500, even with recent underperformance, is down 48.1%. By this point, SCHD is still actually outperforming the Dow Jones Industrial Average, if only slightly. But it is drastically trailing the NASDAQ.

What this shows is that, as I said back in April of last year, during times of extreme volatility, you can expect a short-term outperformance. But in the long run, it is structurally inadequate if your objective is long term upside. In fact, I would even argue that it is inferior to a well-structured high dividend portfolio. Back in September of 2023, I decided to put this to the test. I picked out five companies, which would be AT&T (T), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), Dominion Energy (D), Walgreens Boots Alliance, and Citizens Financial Group (CFG). These were chosen based on a few metrics. First off, I wanted them to be very large corporations that were financially stable. Second, I wanted the portfolio to be relatively diverse. Third, I wanted the yields that these opportunities offered to be high enough to be able to perform at or above the yield that SCHD offers, and finally, I wanted to make sure that none of these holdings were owned by SCHD so I could not be accused of cherry picking the best prospects from the ETF while discarding those that would be most likely to underperform.

As some of you likely know, Walgreens Boots Alliance underperformed significantly. The company was eventually taken private, and I subsequently substituted in Sanofi (SNY) in its place. What is interesting here, however, is that even with that laggard, the portfolio that I constructed has continued to outperform pretty much everything else over the long haul. As the table above illustrates, it too has underperformed SCHD in the near term. But since I originally chose those stocks, it is up 64.7%. That beats the 37.4% that SCHD has captured, and even outperforms the NASDAQ. I do not believe that excluding the true loser of the group is the most appropriate way to check out its total performance. But out of curiosity, I could not resist. Had it not been for the horrible play that was Walgreens Boots Alliance, the portfolio that I constructed would still be up 91.2%, or nearly triple what SCHD has captured while coming in around double with the S&P 500 achieved. Even since my last article in January of this year, the portfolio that I constructed has outperformed the broader market. This shows that a well constructed portfolio with even modest diversification is superior in some instances to the market and is, over the long haul, superior to SCHD.

It is worth noting also that the yield offered up is above what SCHD provides. I know that is a big reason for investors buying into the ETF. As I mentioned earlier, it offers a yield right now of 3.45%. But as the chart above illustrates, the portfolio that I constructed has a yield of 3.97% as of this writing. That's not a big disparity, and it certainly is not as big as it was in the past. However, it is hefty enough to be attractive to yield chasing investors.

Takeaway

If you believe that the short term is going to be painful for the market, which I actually do, I can understand why, on a short-term basis, you would actually gravitate toward SCHD. It has clearly proven itself useful in that regard as I predicted that it could back in April of last year. However, if you are also a long-term investor like I am, you will separate out what could be a short term impact of the ETF from the long term reality that it faces. At the end of the day, I prefer to focus on the long haul since that is what matters most. So even though I acknowledge that short term outperformance could continue, I believe that the long-term orientation necessitates that I reaffirm SCHD as a Sell.

data.title: AIPO: Positioned For Growth Across The AI Value Chain
data.description:
Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (AIPO) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with a diversified investment approach to allocating to the broader AI value chain, including sectors like energy, information technology, industrials, and utilities. The strategy presents a differentiated thematic model that can be exceptionally appealing to those that wish to invest in the AI theme while gaining exposure to the various industries that are poised for elevated growth over the coming years. With the market presenting durability across the theme, I am recommending AIPO with a Buy rating.

I had previously covered AIPO as an ETF Educational article in October 2025, which can be found here.

Investment Thesis

AIPO presents an appealing investment outlay across multiple growth vectors, many of which are loosely tied to the AI growth theme, including energy, industrials, utilities, and information technology. What’s most appealing about this ETF is that it considers the entire value chain rather than a single component.

For example, the Index that AIPO benchmarks to, the MarketVector US Listed AI and Power Infrastructure Index, includes companies that participate in the construction & engineering of data center facilities and power infrastructure, the companies that manufacture and source products, parts, & components used by the EPC companies to construct these facilities, the utilities that operate the power infrastructure, the AI hardware providers, and the data center operators. The ETF covers the broader spectrum of the AI theme, creating a unique and appealing investment structure that can benefit from the long-term development of these facilities.

Engineering, Procurement, & Construction

EPC companies are those responsible for providing engineering and construction services in developing the data center facilities and supporting power infrastructure, which may include constructing the power plant, transmission, and distribution infrastructure, as well as installing the substations and switchgear. Companies that participate in this market and are held in AIPO include Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR) at 8.15%, MasTec, Inc. (MTZ) at 2.38%, and Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL) at 1.29%, amongst others. These companies are a critical component to ensure the infrastructure is properly constructed in a timely effort while providing long-term maintenance services for the power infrastructure. For the EPC industry, backlog analysis is a critical factor in assessing the company’s health for future periods. For example, Quanta Services reported a backlog of $44 billion at the end of FY25 for a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33x, meaning that the company took in more orders than it converted into revenue for FY25. This would suggest that the company’s outlook is healthy, positioning the firm for growth going forward.

As for electrical infrastructure and components manufacturing, AIPO holds companies such as Eaton Corp plc (ETN) at 8.37%, GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) at 8.36%, Hubbell (HUBB) at 2.35%, and Itron (ITRI) at 0.65%, amongst others. Taken together in aggregate, these companies are one of the largest beneficiaries of the increasing demand for power infrastructure, benefiting from a tight market environment that may push more favorable pricing for their products and services. These companies, particularly GE Vernova, can be used to gauge market demand given their multi-year backlogs. Specifically, GE Vernova has a backlog that supports 83GW of natural gas power capacity, creating a large opportunity for supporting electrical infrastructure as well as those installing the infrastructure.

As for energy, AIPO provides exposure to Cameco Corporation (CCJ) at 4.75%, Energy Fuels Inc. (UUUU) at 0.64%, and Denison Mines Corp. (DNN) at 0.58%. AIPO’s energy is primarily tied to the nuclear power theme, investing across uranium producers as well as novel nuclear power developers like NuScale Power Corporation (SMR). While the industry is in the early development and revitalization phase, the driving demand for nuclear power is a long-term initiative with natural gas being the medium-term transitionary fuel.

The utilities play a critical role in operating the power infrastructure that supports the AI data centers. The electric utilities industry accounts for roughly 17% of the portfolio weight and is spread across major players like Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG) at 4.40%, Dominion Energy, Inc. (D) at 2.03%, and PG&E Corporation (PCG) at 1.32%.

The information technology sector, which primarily provides supporting gray space & white space infrastructure housed in the data centers, makes up a smaller proportion of the portfolio as a whole while covering some of the top holdings. These companies include Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT) at 7.38%, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) at 3.85%, and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) at 3.72%, amongst others. This corner of the market has experienced mixed investor sentiment since the start of 2026 as the AI theme began being questioned as to whether the growth path is as robust as initially expected. While I believe enterprise AI adoption remains in the early stages of development, the market may have anticipated a more instantaneous transition rather than a long-term project plan. The fact of the matter is that for AI to work, a substantial amount of upfront capital needed to be deployed in order to develop the supporting infrastructure to advance AI development. I believe enterprise adoption will be a slow-burning growth path as traditional & legacy systems are likely to not be replaced but rather, accompanied by AI.

Bottom line, I believe the growth theme is in the early stages of development with a decades-long development period for adding critical power infrastructure to the grid to support the data center development theme. Given that many of the holdings are tied to more traditional sectors, I believe investors may realize slower, long-term equity appreciation rather than extreme short-term volatility, making this portfolio most appropriate for buy-and-hold investors seeking long-term capital growth. With demand signals pointing towards operational growth across the subsectors, I cannot see any structural rationale that supports a decline other than investor sentiment.

About Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF

AIPO was launched on July 24, 2025 on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has a moderate expense ratio of 69bps, comparable to peer sector-focused ETF strategies.

Given that the fund was launched less than a year ago, reviewing the historical track record is a major limiting factor. Despite our limited historical scope, I believe the strategy is positioned to realize appealing growth, combining features found in strategies like the First Trust RBA American Industrial Renaissance® ETF (AIRR) and the Global X US Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE).

One of the few drawbacks of the fund is that AIPO has relatively thin liquidity with $238.41 million in net assets and an average trading volume of $8.18 million in share value, potentially exposing investors to modest spread risk when entering and exiting a position.

Additional Risks to Consider

While AIPO is broadly diversified across subsectors, the overarching theme is tied together under the AI development momentum, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Any slowdown in data center development or stalling of AI adoption may lead to a lower growth path for companies that participate in this theme. A global disruption to the supply chain may impact materials prices and potentially extend out construction projects. For example, a third of the world’s helium production comes from the Middle East, which may significantly impact semiconductor fabrication rates in the coming quarters.

Final Thoughts

AIPO provides investors with an appealing portfolio of companies participating across the AI value chain, including EPC, electrical components, energy producers, utilities, and IT infrastructure and semiconductor providers. I believe the portfolio is well constructed with multiple positive growth catalysts across the subsectors; I am recommending AIPO with a Buy rating as a component of a diversified equity portfolio strategy.

data.title: Allison Transmission: Not A Great Year And 2026 May Not Be Very Different
data.description:
Introduction

It has been over a year since the first time I covered Allison Transmission (ALSN), and now that we have the full year’s results, it is a good time as any to revisit. Since the first article, when I gave it a hold rating, due to an uncertain macroeconomic outlook, the company’s share price advanced 16.6% vs SPY’s (SPY) 11.5%.

The trucking demand remains soft in key markets, with some positives in other segments; however, not enough to offset the negatives fully. Therefore, my initial hold rating stands.

Briefly on Performance

Now that we have the full year under our belt, let’s take a look at the top-line performance. Revenue for FY25 was around $3B, which is down almost 7% y/y. The same issue is still causing the company’s top-line performance to be lackluster. The NA on-highway demand, especially medium-duty and vocational truck builds, is causing it. On the positive side, the defense segment and outside NA sales helped offset the issues slightly.

Going over the company’s margin development in 2025, we can see a mixed bag. Overall, gross profitability stayed strong, but its operating expenses and product mix moved quite a bit over the year. Most of the margins went down at the end of the year, apart from gross margins, which essentially were maintained. Pricing held up pretty well, with contractual passthroughs helping the company maintain and even improve gross margins.

With the decline in the bottom line, we naturally get a decline in other efficiency metrics like ROA and ROE. These are still quite attractive, but they have declined quite a bit from the beginning of the year. Also, the company’s return on total capital took a hit, which is not ideal. The company may be losing its pricing power, and its moat is eroding.

Going over to the company’s balance sheet, ALSN finished the year with around $1.5B in cash and equivalents, against close to $2.9B in long-term debt. In terms of liquidity and solvency, the debt on ALSN’s books is not an issue at all. The company’s interest coverage ratio was around 10x. Its operations can easily cover the current debt obligations. Additionally, the company continues to generate a great amount of cash flow. Cash from operations came in at around $836m, while free cash flow was around $661m, giving the company a free cash flow margin of around 22%. This is quite strong for an industrial company. The prior year’s free cash flow was closer to 17%, so that is quite an improvement. The important part of this is whether ALSN can maintain this level of cash generation in the future.

In my view, 2025 was not the best year for ALSN. Profitability decreased, while revenues continued to suffer due to the NA market’s weakness. All key efficiency metrics took a massive hit because of it, and it doesn’t look like we will see any recovery just yet.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance for 2026

Net sales are going to be in the range of $5.575B-$5.925B. This is a massive jump, but it is artificial because the company recently closed an acquisition of Dana’s Off-highway segment, which added around $2.5B in sales. This business is going to generate sales in the range of $2.55B-$2.75B.

On the EBITDA guidance, the company set a range of $1.365B-$1.515B, or $1.44B at the midpoint. Sales expectations were lower, but analysts expected EBITDA guidance to be around $1.53B. So, that was disappointing. Overall, I don’t see anything impressive here.

Diversification lever

So, we know that the company has been struggling to gain traction due to the weakness in the NA on-highway market. The other segments are just not big enough to help diversify this concentration risk. Additionally, the weakness is lasting longer than anyone expected. The question is, what is the company doing to help diversify its operations? We saw a big hint in the previous section. Yes, the company decided it wants to add an off-highway revenue segment to its portfolio. On-highway appears to be a lot more cyclical, so diversifying away from this truck market is a very good sign. ALSN wants more exposure to off-highway, industrial, and electrified drivetrains.

Off-highway revenues are expected to be around $2.5B for the year. We don’t have the numbers for 2025, but ALSN did provide us with some performance numbers for 2024. Here, we can see that net sales were around $2.7B, down from $3.085B. We can safely say that the off-highway business addition will be decreasing as well. Or it will be flat, given the range ALSN provided. So, off-highway seems to be struggling too. When should we expect these markets to begin recovering? This is a tough question indeed. Many expected it to improve last year, but the tariff fiasco made everything so distorted that no one wants to transport anything, especially not at these prices. With the oil prices skyrocketing due to geopolitical worries, I don’t see how off-highway and on-highway demand is going to improve right now. Trucking operators are the ones who will feel it first due to worse fuel economics.

What we will probably see that will offset some of the downturn again is the increase in defense spending during these trying times. I don’t think it is going to be enough, but any cushion is positive.

The Defense Angle

Even though the defense segment is still relatively small, it has become a lot more relevant to the whole operation. The more growth this segment sees in the future, the higher the margins will get for the company overall, as these contracts tend to be rather lucrative. The segment is also less cyclical than commercial truck demand and, as we saw, helped offset some of that weakness. In 2025, Allison won an $81m Abrams Tank contract and then another $97m Abrams -related contract, helping revenue reach $267m for 2025. The key product in these contracts is the X1100 cross-drive transmission, along with some kits, overhaul, and other technical support. This seems like a long-term recurring revenue relationship rather than a one-off component sale. And that is an attractive business to many investors.

There is no way that the tailwinds of the defense segment are going to help the company to be seen in a better light, given how badly the other segments are performing, but it does help its overall story of diversification. I’m going to be paying attention to this segment a lot more to see how its growth progresses over the next few quarters.

Valuation

For valuation, I went ahead with what analysts were expecting for the first two years, and then the rest of the assumptions will be anchored there. Below are these assumptions.

We can see revenue will surge by 91% due to the acquisition, and then I don’t think there will be anything exciting. If anything, I may be too optimistic by assuming there will be growth in the first place. For margins, I am also assuming there will be accretion since there should be some synergies and cost savings, as well as the change in mix. The rest of the model, I’m taking the company’s historical averages (more or less). Additionally, I am taking a 10% discount, which is close to the company’s WACC, and I’m using 2.5% for my terminal growth rate. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we come to the following intrinsic value for ALSN.

Investor Takeaway

So, it seems the company is trading close to its fair value currently, and given that I am not particularly excited about the company’s core operations right now, I am maintaining a hold rating and wouldn’t look to add to a position if you already own it. There isn’t a lot of growth to be had just yet, and I would like to see how the addition of the off-highway segment is going to affect ALSN’s overall operations.

Additionally, the defense segment seems to be more interesting, but too small to pay too much attention to. The macroeconomic outlook is still quite jittery, so I wouldn’t be surprised if we see some more corrections ahead.

data.title: QQQI With JEPQ: Outperforming QQQ Amidst The 2026 Tech Correction
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) are two hybrid funds designed to generate double-digit dividend yields whilst maintaining exposure to the growth of stocks included in the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX). In view of the specific nature of the funds’ strategies, a pressing issue is the existence of a "growth ceiling," as a result of which actual returns lag behind the index during periods of active market growth. But 2026 has been marked by a period of heightened market volatility, leading to a decline in the sector’s value and showing signs of a fairly deep technical correction (NDX has fallen by as much as 13.1% since the high of the year). In the context of such market conditions, such funds as QQQI and JEPQ demonstrate more favorable results, mitigating the decline compared to the benchmark—Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ). At the beginning of 2026, total return performance for QQQI and JEPQ was 1.52% and 3.06% better, which confirms their high investment appeal during a period of geopolitical instability and rising inflation risks.

My article's thesis centers on investing in the QQQI and JEPQ funds, both of which have a competitive advantage over the QQQ benchmark. Both funds offer a conservative approach to investing in the Nasdaq-100, offsetting potential drawdowns by generating returns from the premiums of sold options. Even if external circumstances beyond our control and difficult to predict continue to put pressure on these U.S. sectors, a portfolio of QQQI and JEPQ will allow us to partially offset negative price action by continuing to generate double-digit dividend yields. As these funds maintain their exposure to the growth of stocks in these sectors, it allows us to lock in a significant portion of the profits that will emerge from a potential recovery of the Nasdaq-100. In this way, a bet on QQQI and JEPQ allows us to be less dependent on the market phase itself, whilst maintaining various avenues for generating returns.

Forecast Thesis on the Nasdaq-100

This thesis is based on the expectation that the total return profile of QQQI and JEPQ would be more stable than that of QQQ. And this forecast thesis is based on an analysis of the current market conditions of the Nasdaq-100. There is an increase in market volatility, due to the persistence of geopolitical risks stemming from the lack of de-escalation in the Middle East conflict.

Firstly, one should take into account that the VIX index is showing extremely high values, remaining above the 20–22 range since early March 2026 and periodically rising above 30. This index's rise is driven by volatility, enabling QQQI and JEPQ to generate higher premiums from selling options, thereby increasing the funds' overall returns.

Secondly, if the conflict in the Middle East continues, I expect the NDX correction to potentially deepen to 20% (its current maximum drawdown is 13.1%). Unless the situation de-escalates by April 2026, the energy market could impact the economic environment, triggering a new wave of selling in that sector (for example, the Fed could begin signaling tighter monetary policy due to rising inflation risks).

Thirdly, should the NDX extend its correction, an additional 7-10% decline in that sector would result in an automatic loss for the QQQ over the same range. That said, both QQQI and JEPQ employ specific strategies designed to minimize drawdowns, thereby positively impacting their overall return profiles. QQQI, for example, has the ability to generate a more aggressive option premium, because further correction would trigger a rebound in the VIX to the 30 level. That would automatically increase profits from the option strategy, partly offsetting the loss in value of the underlying assets. JEPQ can use its strategy to increase the share of stocks with a lower beta and, similarly, to reduce its drawdown through premium growth by actively managing options. In the end, NDX drawdown is accompanied by increased volatility, the most important component driving the growth in the total returns of QQQI and JEPQ compared to QQQ.

Fundamental Similarities Between QQQI and JEPQ

Because QQQI and JEPQ are both hybrid ETFs designed to generate high cash flow with exposure to growth stocks, the two funds share many fundamental similarities.

  • Both funds are benchmarked to the Nasdaq-100, with large-cap stocks from that sector serving as their underlying assets.
  • When necessary, fund managers may employ active dynamic selection as part of their management strategy, enabling them to deviate from the exact index composition to maximize returns and minimize risks.
  • Revenue is generated through the use of an options strategy, in which selling volatility is the primary condition for earning a premium.
  • Under their dividend policy, funds aim to achieve double-digit returns, allowing for monthly distributions.
  • The two funds offer a balance between capital growth and protection, creating a "growth ceiling" but mitigating volatility if the market is in a sideways or bearish trend.

The Fundamental Concept of QQQI

QQQI's focus is on tracking the Nasdaq-100, with the majority of its investments in the Technology sector (50.17% of the total portfolio) and the Communication sector (15.62% of the total portfolio). Mag-7 companies are included in the top 10 holdings. This portfolio consists of 104 holdings, in which the top 10 assets account for 46.68%. By tracking the index, performance is smoothed out, although the potential to generate alpha from the growth of individual stocks is reduced.

Current QQQI dividend yield stands at 15.54%, with the dividend itself having been reduced in February 2025 from $0.64 per share to $0.61 per share. Nevertheless, dividend dynamics remain fairly stable overall, considering that fund managers use an options strategy to generate income. The goal of QQQI is to strike a balance between payouts and capital preservation in order to generate double-digit yield without eroding NAV.

QQQI option strategies are based on Nasdaq-100 index spreads (where options are sold in conjunction with call spreads). Fund managers purchase additional call options to complement the options they have already written, which allows them to generate a premium on the spread. An advantage of this option strategy is the tax-free return of capital, since the options fall under Section 1256. According to Form 8937 for 2025, the share of capital returned in the payout structure was 98.95%.

The Fundamental Concept of JEPQ

JEPQ, like QQQI, tracking the Nasdaq-100 index, uses a different instrument for this purpose—ELNs. They are contracts with JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM) as the counterparty, which allow you to mimic the returns from selling call options. The disadvantages of this approach include counterparty risk (even though I don’t believe JP Morgan Chase will default) and an additional fee, but overall this instrument is effective in practice. This allows JEPQ to generate a double-digit yield of 11.10%, even though the payouts themselves have been quite volatile over the past year (they range from $0.44 to $0.62). Also, the payouts themselves are classified as ordinary income.

Portfolio JEPQ consists of 108 holdings, where the top 10 account for 40.40% of the portfolio. This lower concentration of assets compared to the QQQI is because managers have more flexibility in determining the weighting of individual assets. When stocks appear overbought, the portfolio's allocation to them is reduced, with the freed-up capital reallocated to stocks with greater growth potential. Also, a difference is that the portfolio is not 100% hedged, only the dynamic part, that depends on the market phase. When the bullish market potential is higher, there is less hedging, or vice versa. By using this approach, managers of JEPQ partially offset the "growth ceiling" problem.

Advantages of QQQI and JEPQ

This is especially evident when the market is in a sideways or bearish trend. This ability to generate returns through an options strategy creates a "safety cushion" which partially offsets a decline in the index. Approximate returns from options amount to 10–12% per year, allowing for a monthly offset of up to 1% of the index’s decline. Even when the market is in a sideways trend, funds still generate returns through the time decay of options. As such, in a sideways market, QQQI and JEPQ can demonstrate better results compared to the QQQ benchmark. In addition, if the price of the fund’s shares falls, the investors can reinvest dividends at more favorable levels, thereby increasing the effectiveness of position cost averaging. For the long term, that significantly increases future cash flow from payouts.

As a comparison of ETFs, I suggest referring to the following table.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

The main risk associated with the funds under review relates to potential erosion of NAV if the market enters a phase of a sharp downtrend. Historical performance data for QQQI and JEPQ do not provide sufficient insight into how resilient the funds’ capital is during periods of severe market declines. Considering a 20-30% collapse of the Nasdaq-100 over 6-12 months, the funds will definitely face NAV erosion under such conditions. It will lead to a potential reduction in their payouts and a more difficult capital recovery once the market returns to a phase of steady growth. Another thing to keep in mind is that QQQI and JEPQ are conservative instruments for maintaining exposure to those U.S. sectors, but they are not a complete hedge against a decline in the Nasdaq-100.

Conclusion

I would conclude that QQQI and JEPQ warrant a "Buy" rating, which means they can be considered as part of a personal investment portfolio. The purchase of these funds allows you to maintain a bet on a potential recovery of the Nasdaq-100, but at the same time receive monthly payments compared to investments in the QQQ benchmark. Currently, with high market volatility in these sectors, QQQI and JEPQ are funds that can capitalize on these conditions in the form of higher premiums from options strategies. This is because the higher the market volatility, desto more expensive the options themselves become—and consequently, so do the profits. These funds are therefore an excellent way to find a temporary safe haven amid market uncertainty whilst maintaining double-digit dividend yields.

data.title: Newmont: A Falling Golden Knife Worth Catching Now (Upgrade)
data.description:
Newmont: Finally, A Buying Opportunity To Capitalize

As you are reading this write-up, I guess you probably know that gold mining stocks have been trying to form a bottom for the past couple of weeks. Buyers are stubbornly refuting the selling pressure that threatened to deepen the reversal of the gains accumulated for 2026. At one point, most, if not all, the gains for gold (GLD) were reversed, which also affected gold mining leader Newmont Corporation (NEM).

Precious metals, especially the alluring yellow metal, have been one of the hottest properties for the past year. And for those that mine them, such as Newmont, these are certainly moments to savor and enjoy. Reaping in the benefits linked to high operating leverage in their capital expenditure heavy portfolio, which obviously is a boon in such dominant times for the gold bugs.

So, I think there isn't any shadow of doubt that gold mining investors are relishing this, and probably couldn’t believe that the market is still pricing them at a relative discount when juxtaposed against their long-term averages. And for NEM's 11.5x, it even appears that the market hasn’t quite gotten FOMO over it, especially when paired against that of Barrick Mining’s (B) 10.9x multiple, as well as Agnico Eagle’s (AEM) markedly higher 15x forward multiple.

In other words, I think one could argue that NEM’s multiples might even have potential for a further rerating if it could deliver on bringing down the costs of its portfolio of assets. At the same time, while also lifting its sales volume higher, in line with management’s long-term outlook of hitting 6 million ounces in production.

Yet, I also highlighted in my last update that NEM doesn’t quite sit on the lower end of that AISC cost curve. And obviously, the issue of renewed inflationary pressure on mining has become more prevalent recently due to the ongoing Iranian conflict, and how they can drive up oil prices and pile onto other costs in the value chain.

This is something that Newmont also cautioned in its 10-K, in which their “operations and projects may be adversely affected by rising energy prices or energy shortages.”

Higher Costs Still A Bugbear To Deal With

So, I think the market is clearly trying to price in these uncertainties, especially since a higher inflationary environment might require the Fed to raise interest rates (although far from certain at this point). In addition, a higher inflationary environment could cause NEM’s AISC to continue going higher, of which costs are already anticipated to rise for 2026, of which NEM’s cost structure is clearly not the cheapest, and neither is it the lowest in terms of year over year growth.

So I guess some skepticism has likely been reflected quickly in the market, as NEM fell 30% from its 2025 peak before the recent bottom. I didn’t quite expect the de-rating to happen so quickly, as the external threats, higher inflationary dynamics pressuring gold prices and also plausibly lifting AISC, could have wreaked havoc in investor psyche.

Yet, I don't think dip buyers are complaining, especially since the market appears to have given patient investors a fantastic opportunity to consider doubling down right now, as the gold market and precious metals in general appear to be forming a very nice bottom.

And what’s important is that I think the company has already laid out the projects to not only help secure future higher production, but also is expected to see these flow through to the bottom line before the end of the decade, as seen with the series of scheduled completions from H2 2027 to 2029.

Of course we will need gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) to stay elevated, of which the current uncertainties in the Middle East appear to have dampened sentiments lately. However, with structural bullish drivers largely intact, and coupled with worsening geopolitical tensions not likely to be lowered anytime soon, I don’t quite see how this level of pessimism in gold mining stocks will persist.

And that probably suggests that high free cash flow margins should be the norm through the late 2020s, although the crucial question is whether there could be an intervening downcycle normalization before the next advancing phase could ensue.

Always remember that Newmont is a mining company, and they need to delicately manage and balance their risk/reward, while increasing production at the right time to benefit from the price surges.

But, as a price taker, they don’t quite have control over the gold pricing cycle, right? What they could achieve is managing their cost curve and portfolio or asset base optimization, which I guess NEM has been executing diligently. Yet, the outlook doesn't indicate that NEM falls into the lower half of the cost outlook, and the AISC outlook for 2026 remains relatively on the high side as compared to peers, as discussed earlier.

Therefore, these uncertainties could continue to stymie a more pronounced valuation re-rating. And, it could be further hounded by a return to assessed midcycle pricing, which Morningstar has also emphasized in a recent update.

Margins Expected To Stay Elevated

Nevertheless, NEM’s gross margins are still generally on the high side, lending credence to its ability to manage costs effectively. Free cash flow margins are also roughly in line with the leading peers in the market. And NEM’s 5Y FCF average also supports the conviction needed that the company has performed relatively well through the cycle.

However, the risk of a prolonged downcycle cannot be ruled out, especially since the current FCF margins are already so much higher than what NEM and peers have achieved over the past five years. Is there really much more room for further upside?

In other words, I think the most significant risk right now is a return to a much higher inflationary scenario that could really dampen and hit investing sentiment hard on NEM. I guess that's why it appears to justify why the market has been somewhat lukewarm on its valuation multiple. You get what I mean, right?

Buy Or Sell NEM Stock?

While these concerns are generally valid, I still want to refer to and infer the motivations of dip buyers who decided to pick up the falling knives over the past 3 weeks.

As we can see from the chart above, NEM's $100 level has held resolutely, affirming the support zone since December of last year. So, I think this goes to show that buying sentiments have remained robust at the "right" levels, where accumulation is clearly gleaned.

I'm not saying that the $100 level is not impenetrable, but it at least gives us conviction that buyers are defending that line very hard and very stoutly. And these buyers aren't willing to give it up this quickly (and easily), weighing the risk that runaway inflation shouldn't be the base case right now.

Therefore, it parlays well into the narrative that NEM should be able to manage the production increases constructively through the rest of the decade, helping it to raise sales volume over time in tune with these output expansions.

That should help to keep FCF margins at a relatively high level to bolster multiples expansion subsequently. Moreover, with NEM’s forward P/E of 11.5x well below the 5Y average of 16.6x, I think the message is clear.

The market is already well cognizant of the risks of a potential earnings and FCF downcycle that investors wouldn’t want to be caught unaware and unprepared for if the midcycle price assumptions were to follow through earlier than expected.

We already know that central banks were pressured to sell their holdings, aligning with the steep selloff in gold ETFs observed over the past month or so.

Therefore, investors shouldn’t simply assume that even these rational investors wouldn’t adjust their calculus when needed, when more important (and urgent) imperatives come into play.

In this case, the ambiguities alluded to in the ME conflict will continue to dominate our risk matrix and are unlikely to be resolved anytime soon.

That said, I think the current risk/reward context and price action have broadly accounted for the enunciated risks, making me a lot more confident to return to the bullish side of the ledger for NEM.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Falcon's Beyond - Peeling The Onion
data.description:
Falcon's Beyond (FBYD) develops amusement parks and other entertainment platforms. The company states to have three divisions: 1) Falcon's Beyond Destinations - theme parks, hotels and attractions, 2) Falcon's Beyond Brands - proprietary digital games and consumer products, and 3) Falcon's Creative Group - master planning services for themed entertainment. But most of these divisions' operations are shared investments, accounted for as equity in earnings. Only Falcon's Beyond Brands and a small part of Falcon's Beyond Destinations are consolidated operating businesses.

For 2025, Falcon's Beyond reported consolidated revenues of $14.9M and an operating loss of ($13.4M). Net operating cash outflow was ($24.6M). Below the operating line, the company reported non-cash equity-in earnings of $17.0M, but would have been ($13.0M) excluding a non-recurring investee's Tenerife property sale of $30.0M. Net loss from continuing operations attributable to common shareholders was ($28.3M) versus reported net income of $2.8M that included one-off property sales.

For Q4 2025, the company reported revenues of $6.6M, an operating loss of ($2.0M), and a net loss of ($0.3M).

To the company's credit, 2025 showed some improvement over 2024. Revenues jumped from $6.7M to $14.9M and operating loss narrowed from ($15.9M) to ($13.4M). But net operating cash outflow worsened dramatically from ($12.6M) to ($24.6M).

But the big elephant in the room is the huge overvaluation of $1.47B or 99x revenues. Furthermore, the company's multi-layered capital structure of debt, preferred stock, and Common B stock usurps and/or dilutes most of the value from the publicly-traded Common A shares.

The company has 48.2M Common A shares. In addition, there are Common B shares of 47.9M, convertible Preferred B shares (with an 11% coupon) of 6.7M, and warrants that convert into 1.3M common shares - a total of 104.1M shares. At the current price of $14.10, FBYD has a market cap of $1.47B or 99x revenues.

The company has financial debt of $15.6M and cash of only $1.9M. The company has an $18.1M liability for two lawsuit claims (one case for $7M remaining to be paid within 10 months, and another case pending where the company has set aside an accrual for $11.1M, see recent 10-K footnote 12). Then the company has preferred shares with a liquidation value of $33.6M that pays an 11% coupon. Finally, there are the Class B common shares held by insiders (that are convertible into A). This leaves very little residual value for the quoted common A shares.

Falcon's Beyond derived 45% of 2025 revenues from accounting, legal, human resources, information technology and other support services to the Falcon's Creative Group of which it is a shared owner (see recent 10-K, page 11). These semi-circular revenues to a related entity are labor-intensive sales. Also, in its first partial year of operations, Falcon’s Attractions (part of the consolidated Falcon's Beyond Brands division) generated 28% of 2025 revenue from product sales and services to a themed entertainment operator at a 44% gross margin. These loss-making service revenues or standard product sales (even with a decent 44% gross margin) normally carry a revenue multiple of below 1x, not 99x.

The company had a very rosy presentation (see March 30th press release) of its results that had contributed to a surging stock price recently. Over the last week, FBYD shares had more than doubled from $6.47 to $14.10, while the stock market had been tanking. The bull case may be the improved revenues and narrowed operating losses in 2025 and Q4 versus the prior year. Possibly headlining the larger revenue numbers of the unconsolidated equity affiliates in the news release may have excited investors.

What was obscured was the fact that these equity affiliate revenues are below the operating line, and normally are not categorized as revenues. Furthermore, the bulk of these equity affiliate revenues are from Falcon's Creative Group, which derives 99% of its revenues from only two Saudi Arabian customers (QIC 60% and NMDC 39%, see 10-K, page 11) - and QIC is a 25% owner of Falcon's Creative Group.

Moreover, as mentioned in the previous paragraph above, 45% of consolidated revenues came from Falcon's Creatiive Group, and Falcon's Beyond is a shared owner of Falcon's Creative Group. Thus, Falcon's Beyond is generating 45% of its revenues from a company in which it is a major owner.

When you peel away the onion, there is very little here to justify anything close to a $1.47B market cap in my opinion. Even the exaggerated revenue numbers that include equity affiliate revenues cannot get close to supporting FBYD's bloated market cap. To make matters worse, the company is hemorrhaging cash and its capital structure leaves little value and solvency risk for common A shareholders in my view.

Short interest is fairly low at 2.5%, which is attractive for an extremely overvalued stock like FBYD. The borrow rate is currently around 50%, which is 3x higher than a few weeks ago, but I have seen worse borrow rates for these types of stocks.

FBYD is a compelling fundamental short.
